questions.question: What feed format does `jekyll-feed` actually emit?
questions.explanation: Despite the colloquial 'RSS' label, `jekyll-feed` emits an Atom feed. Every modern feed reader supports both formats, so the distinction rarely matters in practice - but it's worth knowing when validating the output.

questions.question: Your blog has two collections: `posts` (long-form) and `notes` (short snippets). Some readers want only the long-form. What's the cleanest way to support this with `jekyll-feed`?
choices: Tell readers to filter the main feed in their RSS reader
Configure per-collection feeds in `_config.yml` under `feed.collections` so `notes/` gets its own feed path
Manually maintain a second `notes-feed.xml` file outside of Jekyll
Move notes into a separate Jekyll site
questions.explanation: `jekyll-feed` supports per-collection feeds out of the box - add the collection name under `feed.collections` and the plugin emits a separate feed file for it. Readers can then subscribe to one feed without manual filtering.

questions.question: Why is full-content (rather than excerpt-only) usually the right default for an engineering blog's RSS feed?
choices: RSS readers can't render HTML, so excerpts often display incorrectly
Full content lets readers read offline and in their preferred typography, which is much of why they subscribe via RSS in the first place
Search engines penalise sites that don't ship full content in their feed
questions.explanation: Readers choose RSS partly so they can read offline (planes, tunnels) and in the reader app's typography. Excerpt-only feeds force a click-through that defeats both. The other claims are false - RSS readers render HTML fine, search engines don't read feeds for ranking, and autodiscovery is unrelated to content length.
